title: Weakened Starmer: Britain's Government Drifts Left
content:
In Britain, political instability has become a chronic condition with a grimly familiar storyline. A once-triumphant prime minister’s poll ratings plummet, turning Downing Street into an isolated bunker. The humiliation of Sir Keir Starmer, Britain’s fourth prime minister in four years, is perhaps even greater than that of his Conservative predecessors. After securing a landslide victory in 2024 and boasting of governing for a decade, impending local elections threaten to finish his tenure. A prime minister who initially defined himself as a constraint on the left-leaning parliamentary Labour Party now seemingly governs as its hostage.

The revelation that Peter Mandelson was appointed as ambassador to America despite Sir Keir knowing of his ties to Jeffrey Epstein has shattered the prime minister’s image of competence and incorruptibility. Key aides who ran his operation have resigned, and the cabinet secretary is departing. After the drama of the past week, it is tempting to hope that this crisis will prove the catalytic moment Britain needs for genuine reform. However, the more likely path is that the Labour Party will drift toward its soft-left comfort zone, preoccupied by self-preservation. Traumatized by how quickly voters have soured, the party will likely choose unity and caution over boldness.

This political stasis in Downing Street stands in stark contrast to the urgency of the national situation. While economic growth is not dreadful by European standards, it remains too meager to provide voters with the living standards and public services they demand. The cost of servicing Britain’s debt has risen to its highest level since the late 1980s. Rearmament, an aging population, and an unreformed welfare system are severely straining public finances. Voters are keenly aware of these challenges, contributing to a declinist mood redolent of Britain in the 1970s.

Sir Keir’s election landslide was intended to help the country escape this exact trap, yet he has largely failed due to a lack of political capital and a concrete plan. Labour’s safety-first campaign promised small giveaways while ruling out major tax changes or structural reforms. The party drew up laws to empower trade unions and renationalize railways, but no intellectual spadework was done on reforming the civil service, regulated markets, or welfare. Many incoming MPs expected money to flow freely, and when they revolted against cuts to welfare and pensioner benefits, Sir Keir ultimately backed down. His cautious approach in opposition has proven to be a remarkably hollow project for actual governance.

Because a change of course now seems highly improbable, powerful currents will inevitably pull Labour further left. Sir Keir’s boasts of having purged the hard left mask how far the party's center of gravity has shifted since the days of Tony Blair. The leadership contenders most popular within the party are all to the left of Sir Keir, and 89% of party members believe taxes and spending should increase. To hold on to power and fend off right-wing populists, Labour must attract voters who have defected to the Green Party. The heaviest cost of this strategy will be what is left undone, as vital reforms to welfare, education, and public services are repeatedly sidelined.
